cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference). If an attacker can supply a graph where an Identity node's input references a non-existent node name, this triggers a segfault.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE: a null pointer dereference in IsIdentityConsumingSwitch when graph.GetNode returns nullptr for a missing node, which is then dereferenced via IsSwitch(*input_node). The CWE, location, and impact all align with CVE-2022-23589.

actual_issue_summary: In IsIdentityConsumingSwitch (and a similar location in constant folding), graph.GetNode() can return nullptr when the graph is missing expected nodes (e.g., from a maliciously altered SavedModel). The nullptr is then dereferenced without a check, causing a crash. The fix adds a nullptr check before dereferencing.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The Grappler optimizer in TensorFlow can be used to cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that `IsSimplifiableReshape` would trigger `CHECK` failures. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: GetNode returns a potentially null pointer for node.input(1), which is then immediately dereferenced in IsReallyConstant(*new_shape) without a null check, causing a null pointer dereference and crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash. An attacker who can supply a graph where a Reshape node's second input references a node name not present in the node_map_ can trigger a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: node_map_->GetNode(node.input(1)) returns NULL if the node name is not found

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: new_shape is NULL because the node was not found in the map

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-23581) is specifically about TF_CHECK_OK being used on MakeShape calls, which causes CHECK failures (process abort) when MakeShape returns an error status due to invalid shapes in a crafted SavedModel. The fix replaces TF_CHECK_OK with proper error propagation. This finding describes a different potential issue (null pointer dereference on GetNode result), which is not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: TF_CHECK_OK was used on TensorShapeUtils::MakeShape calls in IsSimplifiableReshape. If MakeShape returned a non-OK status (e.g., due to invalid shape dimensions in a crafted SavedModel), TF_CHECK_OK would trigger a CHECK failure and crash the process. The fix replaces TF_CHECK_OK with proper error status propagation.

cve_desc: A Vulnerability of LG Electronic web OS TV Emulator could allow an attacker to escalate privileges and overwrite certain files. This vulnerability is due to wrong environment setting. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability through crafted configuration files and executable files.

undesired_operation.description: When argc == 9 and argv[8] equals "*", arg_start is set to 9. The subsequent access argv[arg_start][0] (i.e., argv[9][0]) reads out of bounds because valid indices are 0 through argc-1 (0 through 8).
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that can lead to a crash (denial of service) or potentially information disclosure. An attacker on the IRC network can send a crafted WHO reply to trigger this.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: argc is 9, so 9 < 8 is false; execution continues past the early return.
argc is 9, so 9 > 8 is true; we enter the block.

argv[arg_start][0] is accessed, i.e., argv[9][0]. Since argc==9, valid indices are 0..8, so argv[9] is out of bounds.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed in the commit: when argc==9 and argv[8]=="*", arg_start becomes 9 and argv[9] is accessed out of bounds. The fix adds an (argc > 9) check before setting arg_start to 9. The commit message confirms this is a crash from a malformed message 352.

actual_issue_summary: When a malformed IRC 352 (WHO) message has exactly 9 arguments with argv[8]=="*", arg_start is set to 9 without checking argc > 9, causing an out-of-bounds array access on argv[9] leading to a crash. The fix adds the (argc > 9) check before allowing arg_start to be set to 9.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

When the key is missing from the outputs, this dereferences an end() iterator, causing a null pointer dereference / crash. The fix adds a check that the inner find() succeeded before dereferencing.

When a NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node is subsequently encountered with len > 0, pop_n(len*2) underflows the internal stack pointer (cursp), corrupting the code generator's stack state and leading to out-of-bounds memory access in subsequent operations.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack pointer underflow in the code generator leads to generation of bytecode referencing invalid register indices, potentially causing out-of-bounds memory writes during VM execution, leading to code execution or crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same bug fixed in the commit. The fix adds 'val &&' checks before pop_n/pop operations to prevent stack pointer underflow when val is 0. The CVE's CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) is a downstream consequence of the stack underflow identified by the finding. The finding accurately describes the root cause and mechanism.

Since codegen with val=0 doesn't push values onto the stack, these pops underflow the internal stack pointer, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds 'val &&' guards to both conditional blocks.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The overflow check uses an `int` (32-bit) variable `new_num_elements` to store the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` which returns `int64_t`. The 64-bit result is silently truncated to 32 bits, so a large product that is negative in 64-bit (indicating overflow) can truncate to a non-negative 32-bit int, bypassing the overflow check. This allows construction of a TensorShape with crafted large dimensions, leading to downstream memory issues.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential memory corruption — attacker provides a crafted sparse_shape that bypasses the overflow check, causing TensorShape construction or later operations to use an inconsistent shape.

MultiplyWithoutOverflow returns int64_t but result is stored in 32-bit int, causing truncation. A negative int64_t value (overflow signal) may become non-negative after truncation to 32 bits.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue in CVE-2022-23568: the faulty overflow check using a 32-bit int variable to hold a 64-bit result from MultiplyWithoutOverflow, which allows the overflow to go undetected. The fix replaces this entire manual overflow check with TensorShape::BuildTensorShape which properly validates dimensions. The CWEs (CWE-190 integer overflow, CWE-681 incorrect conversion) are consistent with the CVE's CWE-190.

actual_issue_summary: The overflow check in AddManySparseToTensorsMap used an `int` (32-bit) variable `new_num_elements` to accumulate the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` (which returns `int64_t`), causing silent truncation that could bypass the overflow detection. Even if the check passed, `TensorShape` was constructed directly from user input without safe validation. The fix removes the faulty manual overflow check and replaces the direct TensorShape constructor call with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape` which returns a Status on invalid/overflowing dimensions.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

undesired_operation.description: The loop assigns rb_stats, rb_voip_mtc, etc. based solely on the block type byte (rb_hdr->bt) without verifying that rb_len (or remaining packet bytes) is large enough to cover the full structure. Later, the code dereferences many fields deep into these structures (e.g., rb_stats->jitter_dev, rb_voip_mtc->jb_abs_max), reading beyond the bounds of the actual packet buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from a network-controlled packet. An attacker can craft an RTCP XR packet with a valid block type but a small rb_len, causing the code to read past the packet buffer. This can lead to information disclosure of adjacent heap/stack memory, or a crash (denial of service).

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the CVE-2021-43845 vulnerability: the RTCP XR block data fields are not validated against the received packet size before being cast and dereferenced, leading to out-of-bound reads. The fix adds exactly these bounds checks, and the finding's CWE-125 matches the CVE's CWE.

actual_issue_summary: In pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rx_rtcp_xr(), when parsing RTCP XR report blocks, the code casts rb_hdr to specific block structure types (rb_rr_time, rb_dlrr, rb_stats, rb_voip_mtc) without checking that the remaining packet data is large enough to contain the full structure. This allows an attacker to send a crafted RTCP XR packet with blocks whose declared length is smaller than the structure size, causing out-of-bounds reads when the fields are later accessed. The fix adds bounds checks verifying that rb_hdr + sizeof(structure) <= pkt + size before each assignment.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_list_del function in list.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

This allows extent_count (up to 65535) iterations that each allocate a GF_ItemExtentEntry without consuming any box size, enabling uncontrolled memory allocation.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via excessive memory allocation. An attacker crafts a malicious ISOBMFF file causing allocation of up to 65535 GF_ItemExtentEntry structs per location entry without any size-based limit, exhausting memory.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a double-free vulnerability (CWE-415) caused by using gf_malloc (which doesn't zero-initialize memory) instead of GF_SAFEALLOC (calloc-based, zero-initializes). When gf_malloc is used, struct fields like extent_entries contain garbage values. During cleanup (e.g., on error paths or destruction), gf_list_del is called on these uninitialized pointers, causing a double-free. The finding instead describes a resource exhaustion / uncontrolled memory allocation issue, which is a different vulnerability class entirely.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a double-free (CWE-415) caused by using gf_malloc instead of GF_SAFEALLOC (calloc) when allocating GF_ItemLocationEntry and GF_ItemExtentEntry structs. gf_malloc does not zero-initialize memory, so fields within these structs (e.g., extent_entries list pointers) contain uninitialized/garbage values. When the box destructor later attempts to free these entries (calling gf_list_del on garbage pointers), it leads to a double-free, causing a denial of service. The fix replaces gf_malloc with GF_SAFEALLOC which zero-initializes the allocated memory.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can read from outside of bounds of heap allocated data by sending specially crafted illegal arguments to `tf.raw_ops.SdcaOptimizerV2`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/sdca_internal.cc#L320-L353) does not check that the length of `example_labels` is the same as the number of examples. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a4e138660270e7599793fa438cd7b2fc2ce215a6. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The loop iterates num_examples times (derived from example_weights.size()), but accesses example_labels(example_id) without verifying that example_labels has at least num_examples elements. If the example_labels tensor is smaller than the example_weights tensor, this results in an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to information disclosure of heap/memory contents or crash (denial of service). An attacker can craft a TensorFlow graph where example_labels has fewer elements than example_weights.

actual_issue_summary: The function Examples::Initialize does not validate that example_labels has the same number of elements as example_weights (num_examples). When example_labels is shorter, the loop accessing example_labels(example_id) reads out of bounds from heap memory.

cve_desc: Memory leak in the afra_box_read function in MP4Box in GPAC 1.0.1 allows attackers to read memory via a crafted file.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about memory leaks: when ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE fails mid-loop, allocated memory (ae) is not yet inserted into the list and thus leaked. The fix moves gf_list_insert before ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE so that on error, the allocated entry is in the list and gets freed during cleanup. The finding describes uncontrolled allocation/DoS from missing validation of global_entry_count, which is a different issue from the memory leak fixed by the commit.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in afra_box_read: when ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE macro returns an error mid-loop, the just-allocated entry (ae) has not yet been inserted into the list (local_access_entries or global_access_entries), so it is leaked. The fix moves gf_list_insert to immediately after allocation, before any ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE checks, ensuring the entry is tracked and freed on error.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

If depth exceeds the input tensor's last dimension, chip<0>(i) performs an out-of-bounds read.

This allows null pointer dereference (empty input) or out-of-bounds heap access.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: A negative int value from ctx->input(0) is implicitly cast to std::size_t (unsigned), wrapping to a very large value, which is then passed to buf->Peek() potentially causing an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory read leading to information disclosure or denial of service via crash. An attacker providing a negative integer input causes an enormous unsigned index to be used.

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with a negative integer scalar value

step_by_step_execution.description: The scalar value is read from ctx->input(0) as int and implicitly cast to std::size_t
buf->Peek is called with the wrapped large index

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-29195) is specifically about the lack of validation that the input tensor is a scalar before calling .scalar<int>()(). Passing a non-scalar tensor triggers a CHECK-failure causing denial of service. The finding instead describes a signed-to-unsigned integer conversion issue (negative value wrapping), which is a different vulnerability mechanism. While both issues involve the same line of code that was removed in the fix, the finding does not describe the actual CVE, which is about missing shape validation (scalar check), not about sign conversion.

actual_issue_summary: The StagePeek op assumes ctx->input(0) is a scalar tensor and calls .scalar<int>()() without first validating the tensor's shape. If a non-scalar tensor is provided, this triggers an internal CHECK-failure, causing a denial of service crash. The fix removes the index/Peek logic entirely, replacing it with a Get() call that doesn't require the index input.

When xQueueSizeInBytes is large enough, adding sizeof(Queue_t) causes the sum to wrap around, resulting in pvPortMalloc allocating a buffer that is far too small. cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read from fast array backing store. The object length is captured early, but subsequent njs_value_to_integer() calls on user-supplied arguments can trigger valueOf callbacks that modify the source array (e.g., shrinking it). The captured length becomes stale, causing 'delete' (computed from the stale length) to exceed the array's actual current length, resulting in reading past the allocated array->start buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read leading to information disclosure of adjacent heap memory or crash. If the array shrinks significantly, many elements past the array's actual bounds are read and copied into the newly created deleted array, which is returned to the caller.

judgments.reasoning: The actual vulnerability is about uninitialized elements in the 'deleted' fast array when 'this' is a slow/non-fast array with gaps. When njs_value_property_i64() returns non-OK (property not found, i.e., a gap), the corresponding deleted->start[i] is left uninitialized. Later enumeration of the deleted array (e.g., in njs_value_own_enumerate) dereferences these uninitialized values, causing a segfault (NULL pointer dereference). The fix adds `njs_set_invalid(&deleted->start[i])` for these gap entries. The finding instead describes a speculative OOB read from stale array length due to valueOf callbacks, which is an entirely different issue not addressed by the fix.

actual_issue_summary: When Array.prototype.splice() is called on a non-fast-array 'this' that has gaps (missing properties), and the resulting 'deleted' array is a fast array, the deleted array's backing store entries corresponding to the gaps are left uninitialized. Later operations (like enumeration in njs_value_own_enumerate) that traverse the deleted array encounter these uninitialized values, causing a segmentation fault (NULL dereference). The fix initializes these gap entries with njs_set_invalid().

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. While the finding focuses on the underflow scenario rather than the scalar-tensor scenario specifically, it correctly identifies the same vulnerable code point and the same class of impact (heap OOB read). The fix adds the dims() > 0 check that prevents both the scalar access and the scenario the finding describes.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() is called which decrements the reference count to 0 and deletes the object. Then when the function returns (via OP_REQUIRES), the unique_ptr destructor also calls delete on the same pointer, causing a double-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or potentially arbitrary code execution through heap corruption caused by double-free

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly describes the CVE. The unique_ptr manages the resource, but Unref() also frees it when refcount hits 0. When the unique_ptr goes out of scope, it double-frees the already-freed object. The fix adds result.release() after Unref() to prevent the unique_ptr from freeing it again. CWE-415 (double-free) is essentially the same issue as CWE-416 (use-after-free) listed in the CVE — the CVE description itself mentions both the double-free and the use-after-free during cleanup.

undesired_operation.description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the raw pointer obtained from result.release() is never freed, causing a memory/resource leak. Repeated exploitation leads to denial of service via resource exhaustion.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service through memory exhaustion by repeatedly triggering the ALREADY_EXISTS path

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential memory leak in the ALREADY_EXISTS path, which is unrelated to CVE-2021-37652. The CVE is about the double-free/use-after-free caused by Unref() plus unique_ptr destructor in the InitFromSerialized failure path. The memory leak issue is a different bug not addressed by this CVE or its fix.

actual_issue_summary: When InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() decrements the refcount to 0 and frees the resource. However, since 'result' is a unique_ptr, when it goes out of scope its destructor attempts to free the same memory again, causing a use-after-free/double-free. The fix adds result.release() after Unref() to relinquish the unique_ptr's ownership of the already-freed memory.

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

undesired_operation.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash digests. strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0') in either operand, so if the computed hash (passwd1_sum) and the stored hash (key/passwd2_sum) both contain a null byte at the same early position, strncmp returns 0 (match) without comparing the remaining bytes. This allows authentication bypass with far less effort than a full hash collision.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker can find a password that passes the check without matching the full hash. If the stored hash has a null byte at position N, the attacker only needs to brute-force N bytes of hash output (e.g., if the first byte is null, any password whose hash also starts with null is accepted — roughly 1 in 256 attempts).

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37848) is specifically about timing side-channel information leakage due to using strncmp for hash comparison (CWE-200). The finding identifies the same problematic code (strncmp calls) but describes a different vulnerability: null-byte truncation causing authentication bypass (CWE-187). While both issues stem from using strncmp on binary data, and both are fixed by the same commit, the CVE's described vulnerability is about timing leakage, not null-byte truncation. The finding describes a different security consequence than what the CVE documents.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a timing side-channel in password hash comparison. strncmp was used to compare password hashes, which leaks timing information because it returns early on the first differing byte. An attacker could observe response times to deduce hash bytes incrementally. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq, a constant-time comparison function.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

In mruby's VM, when cibase->n is set to a value >= 15, the VM expects arguments to be in an array object, but the vulnerable code just copies them individually onto the stack. This mismatch causes a NULL pointer dereference when the VM tries to access the expected array. The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in len+2, which is not the actual vulnerability. The fix adds handling for len >= 15 by creating an array, not by preventing integer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: When a fiber is resumed/switched with 15 or more arguments, the vulnerable code copies them individually onto the stack and sets cibase->n = len. In mruby's VM convention, n >= 15 signals that arguments are packed in an array. Since the arguments weren't actually packed into an array, the VM later dereferences a NULL/invalid pointer expecting an array object, causing a NULL pointer dereference. The fix packs 15+ arguments into an array and sets len = 15 to match the VM's expectations.

cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. For a match found at position `pos` from the haystack start, `count` equals `pos`, so the suffix size is set to `pos + size - 1`. The correct suffix size should be `haystack_size - pos - size` (i.e., `count_initial - count`). The excess is `(size - 1)` bytes beyond the actual string end for every byte the match is from the end, allowing an out-of-bounds read past the original string buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — an attacker invoking the PostScript `rsearch` operator can obtain a string reference whose size extends past the end of the original string's allocation, allowing reading of adjacent heap/memory contents.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2020-15900: the incorrect size calculation `count + (size - 1)` for the 'post' string in backward (rsearch) mode. The CVE description explicitly states 'The rsearch calculation for the post size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t.' The fix replaces this calculation with the correct `op[-3].tas.rsize -= count + size` for the reverse case. The finding's CWE-131 (incorrect calculation of buffer size) aligns well with the actual bug, and while the CVE lists CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write / memory corruption), the finding's CWE-125 describes a closely related consequence of the same miscalculation.

This produced a size that was too large and could underflow to UINT32_MAX (since the variables are unsigned), leading to memory corruption. The fix separates the forward and backward cases, correctly computing the pre and post string sizes for each direction.

cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. While the finding focuses on the out-of-bounds read aspect (CWE-125) rather than the stack write/corruption aspect (CWE-120) that occurs when strchr finds a stale '\n' in uninitialized memory, it describes the same root cause: missing null termination after memcpy leading to strchr operating on uninitialized stack data. This is a valid component of the CVE.

When found, that byte is overwritten with '\0', corrupting stack data. The fix replaces memcpy with strscpy to ensure null termination, and only overwrites '\n' if actually found within the properly terminated string.

--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -45,12 +45,13 @@
 	p = NameBuff;
 	len = (int)STRLEN(p);
 
-	if (bt_help(wp->w_buffer)
+	if ((bt_help(wp->w_buffer)

 #endif
-		|| bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
-		|| wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
+		    || bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
+		    || wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
+		&& len < MAXPATHL - 1)

undesired_operation.description: The statement *(p + len++) = ' ' writes a space character at offset len into NameBuff without checking that len < MAXPATHL. If the translated buffer name is long enough, this writes past the NUL terminator or out-of-bounds. Subsequently, vim_snprintf is called with MAXPATHL - len as the size parameter, which underflows to a huge size_t value when len > MAXPATHL, enabling a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow via a crafted long buffer name. An attacker who can control the buffer name (e.g. by opening a file with a very long path containing many control characters that expand via trans_characters) can corrupt heap memory, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

vim_snprintf is called with size MAXPATHL - len = 0, but the string is no longer NUL-terminated within bounds, leading to potential out-of-bounds access

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

undesired_operation.description: When setuid() fails, execution continues past the error handling (which only exits for EAGAIN when EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is defined). The function then unconditionally sets current_user.euid = current_user.uid and current_user.egid = current_user.gid, making the program believe privileges were dropped when they were not. The process remains running with elevated (e.g., root) effective uid.
undesired_operation.impact: The program continues operating with elevated privileges while internal state records that privileges were dropped. Subsequent security decisions based on current_user.euid will be incorrect, potentially allowing privileged operations that should have been denied.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2019-18276 is not about setuid() failing. It's about setuid() succeeding but only dropping the effective UID, not the saved UID, on Linux systems that support saved UIDs. This allows an attacker to later call setuid() again (e.g., via a loaded shared object) to regain the original privileged UID. The fix uses setresuid() which explicitly sets real, effective, AND saved UIDs. The finding describes a different hypothetical issue (error handling on setuid failure) that is not the vulnerability being fixed.

actual_issue_summary: The disable_priv_mode() function used setuid()/setgid() to drop privileges, but on Linux systems supporting saved UIDs, these calls only drop the effective UID/GID, not the saved UID/GID. An attacker could regain privileges by calling setuid() with the saved UID (e.g., via 'enable -f' to load a shared object). The fix uses setresuid()/setresgid() to explicitly set all three UIDs/GIDs (real, effective, and saved) to the unprivileged values.

cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The fix replaces DCHECK with a proper runtime bounds check that logs a warning and returns early.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The pointer `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If the loop over `train_nodes` finds no dequeue op, `dequeue_node` remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check evaluates an indeterminate pointer value (undefined behavior), and if the garbage value is non-null, `dequeue_node->name()` dereferences it, causing a crash or worse.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash, or potential code execution through dereference of an uninitialized pointer pointing to attacker-influenced stack memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: dequeue_node is declared as a raw pointer without initialization

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If no node in train_nodes is a dequeue op, the loop completes without setting dequeue_node, leaving it uninitialized.
Since dequeue_node holds an indeterminate value, evaluating it is undefined behavior. If the garbage value happens to be non-null, the branch is taken.

step_by_step_execution.description: dequeue_node->name() is called, dereferencing the uninitialized pointer

in_state.value: uninitialized (indeterminate garbage value interpreted as non-null)

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-41225: the uninitialized `dequeue_node` pointer in the Grappler optimizer, which the fix initializes to nullptr.

actual_issue_summary: In TensorFlow's Grappler AutoParallel::Initialize, the `dequeue_node` pointer was declared without initialization. If no Dequeue node exists in `train_nodes`, the pointer remains uninitialized, leading to undefined behavior when it is subsequently checked and potentially dereferenced. The fix initializes it to `nullptr`.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The variable 'depth' is derived from input_min_vec.NumElements() but is never validated against the actual channel dimension of the 4D input tensor (dim_size(3) for NHWC). The check 'input_min_vec.dim_size(0) == depth' is tautological for 1D tensors. When the scales vector size mismatches the channel dimension expected by the mkldnn reorder (mask=2 targets the channel dimension in NCHW ordering), mkldnn will read out-of-bounds from the scales vector.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from the scales vector during mkldnn reorder execution, potentially leading to denial of service (crash) or information disclosure of heap contents.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE describes incomplete validation in MklRequantizePerChannelOp that allows out-of-bounds access and undefined behavior. The finding correctly identifies that input validation is insufficient - specifically the lack of proper validation between depth (from input_min_vec) and the actual tensor dimensions. This is consistent with the CVE description stating the implementation 'does not perform full validation for all the input arguments.' The fix reorganizes validation to occur before data access and adds additional checks, which aligns with the finding's identification of CWE-20 (improper input validation) and CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read).

actual_issue_summary: The MklRequantizePerChannelOp::Compute function had incomplete input validation: (1) validation checks occurred after data was already accessed (e.g., accessing flat<float>()(0) on input_requested_min/max before verifying they are scalars), allowing null pointer dereference or OOB access; (2) missing validation that input_requested_min and input_requested_max are scalar tensors; (3) DCHECK (debug-only) used instead of OP_REQUIRES for the negative min check, meaning the check was absent in production builds.

undesired_operation.impact: Potential memory corruption or undefined behavior — if r_charset_encode_str interprets the negative/wrapped outlen as unsigned, it may mishandle bounds checking. An attacker who can set the RABIN2_CHARSET environment variable and provide a binary with a large section (>536MB) can trigger this.

The finding describes a completely different issue in the RABIN2_CHARSET code path involving outlen overflow, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in needle-based pointer arithmetic during string scanning. Specifically: (1) 'needle + 4 < to' could overflow when needle is near UT64_MAX, changed to 'needle < to - 4'; (2) 'buf + needle - from' evaluated left-to-right could overflow, fixed by parenthesizing as 'buf + (needle - from)'; (3) added 'needle < UT64_MAX - 4' guard to the while loop. These overflows caused out-of-bounds reads from the allocated buffer.

cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

undesired_operation.description: Multiple memcpy calls write attacker-controlled lengths of data into the fixed-size stack buffer data_signed[200] without any bounds checking. The total written is 1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len, which can easily exceed 200 bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker can craft a FIDO U2F attestation with a large credential_id to overflow the buffer and overwrite the return address.

memcpy copies rpid_hash_len bytes into data_signed at offset 1
memcpy copies client_data_hash (32 bytes) into data_signed
memcpy copies credential_id (attacker-controlled length) into data_signed. If credential_id_len is large (e.g., 200), this overflows the 200-byte buffer.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With credential_id_len being attacker-controlled and potentially large, the total offset exceeds the 200-byte buffer size, causing a stack buffer overflow.

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2021-40818: a buffer overflow in data_signed[200] during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration. The fix replaces the fixed-size stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to the actual data lengths.

actual_issue_summary: The fixed-size stack buffer data_signed[200] in check_attestation_fido_u2f could overflow when the combined size of rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, and cert_y exceeded 200 bytes. The fix replaces it with a dynamically allocated buffer sized exactly to the needed length.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

A crafted STUN message with many small attributes causes writes beyond the array, resulting in a stack buffer overflow. The fix adds a bounds check (msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt) to the loop condition.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: No validation that in_batch == out_batch or in_depth == out_depth. When in_batch * in_rows * in_cols is smaller than what the out_batch/out_rows/out_cols loops expect, the computed in_index exceeds the allocated in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer, causing heap buffer overflow write.
code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
undesired_operation.impact: Heap out-of-bounds write leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service. Attacker provides mismatched orig_input_tensor_shape and out_backprop dimensions.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about missing validation that input tensor dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth) can be zero, leading to empty buffer creation and subsequent out-of-bounds access. The finding identifies OOB access on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat at the same code location. When dimensions are zero, the buffer is empty (size 0) but the loops driven by out_backprop dimensions still attempt writes - this is a specific case of the 'smaller than expected' scenario the finding describes. The finding correctly identifies the vulnerable code path and the consequence (heap OOB access), even though it frames it as a dimension mismatch rather than specifically zero dimensions.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation does not validate that the input tensor shape dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth) are non-zero. When any dimension is 0, an empty EigenDoubleMatrixMap is created but then accessed with out-of-bounds indices derived from the output backprop tensor dimensions, leading to heap buffer out-of-bounds access. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring all four dimensions are non-zero.

cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service. An attacker controlling the PORT command argument can craft values that overwrite the return address or other stack data.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read. An attacker can craft a NE binary with a segnum value up to 255, reading segment_entries far beyond its allocated size, potentially leaking sensitive heap data or causing a crash.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

If ts_fidx is already 0, this causes an invalid (negative/underflow) index, leading to an out-of-bounds read. The fix adds a check `&& sp->ts_fidx > 0` before the decrement.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

If that byte happens to be whitespace, pnew is decremented below the allocated buffer, and '*pnew = NUL' writes before the allocated buffer (out-of-bounds write / heap corruption).

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary kernel memory write of the constant 0x01000000 at a controlled negative offset from buffer->ptr, leading to kernel memory corruption, denial of service, or potential privilege escalation.

While the CVE description emphasizes the heap overflow via copy_from_user, the buffer->ptr[words] write is another direct consequence of the same root cause (the int truncation). The fix is exactly changing 'int words' to 'size_t words'.

actual_issue_summary: In pxa3xx_gcu_write, the 'count' parameter (size_t) is divided by 4 and stored in 'words' (int), causing integer overflow/truncation. This allows bypassing the size check (words >= PXA3XX_GCU_BATCH_WORDS) and leads to a heap overflow via copy_from_user and an out-of-bounds write via buffer->ptr[words]. The fix changes 'words' from int to size_t.

The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

If the total pixel width of consecutive partial-coverage spans exceeds the size of r->_buf, a heap buffer overflow occurs.

cve_desc: ADMesh through 0.98.4 has a heap-based buffer over-read in stl_update_connects_remove_1 (called from stl_remove_degenerate) in connect.c in libadmesh.a.

The commit fix has two parts: (1) in stl_remove_degenerate, adding checks that neighbor2 != -1 / neighbor1 != -1 before calling stl_update_connects_remove_1, and (2) adding facet_num < 0 check inside stl_update_connects_remove_1 itself. The finding describes exactly the caller-side missing validation that allows -1 to be passed as facet_num, leading to the heap-based buffer over-read (CWE-125/CWE-129).

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2018-25033 is a heap-based buffer over-read in stl_update_connects_remove_1. The function could be called with facet_num=-1 from stl_remove_degenerate when both neighbor1 and neighbor2 are -1, causing an out-of-bounds array access on stl->neighbors_start[facet_num]. The fix adds both caller-side checks (ensuring the neighbor value isn't -1 before passing it) and callee-side validation (returning early if facet_num < 0).

cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2019-15691) is about stack use-after-return due to incorrect cleanup in ZRLEDecoder via removeUnderlying(). The fix changes the cleanup from removeUnderlying() to flushUnderlying() + setUnderlying(NULL, 0) to avoid accessing freed stack memory when exceptions occur. The finding describes a completely different issue (uninitialized palette access) that is unrelated to the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a stack use-after-return in ZlibInStream. When ZRLEDecoder calls zis->removeUnderlying(), the ZlibInStream's destructor or subsequent operations could access the stack-allocated buffer (buf) or the underlying stream after they are no longer valid (e.g., after an exception unwinds the stack). The fix replaces removeUnderlying() with flushUnderlying() + setUnderlying(NULL, 0) to safely detach from the underlying stream without risking access to invalid memory.

cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. This allows local attackers to perform symlink attacks to gain privileges of other users. cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. A buffer of only MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE is then allocated, and memcpy copies the old (larger) actions_len bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read leading to information disclosure or crash (denial of service). An attacker-controlled RFB server can send a crafted nSubrects value to trigger this.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` that bypasses the buffer size check, which is exactly the vulnerability fixed in CVE-2018-20020. The fix changes the check to `hdr.nSubrects > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE / (4 + (BPP / 8))` to avoid the overflow. While the finding emphasizes over-read (CWE-125) rather than the CVE's out-of-bound write (CWE-787), it correctly identifies the same root cause (CWE-190 integer overflow) and the same vulnerable code path. The loop also writes via GotFillRect with out-of-bounds coordinates, so both read and write issues exist.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` in the CoRRE handler allows the buffer size check to be bypassed when a malicious server sends a large nSubrects value. This leads to heap out-of-bounds write via the subsequent loop processing. The fix changes the check to use division (`hdr.nSubrects > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE / (4 + (BPP / 8))`) to avoid the overflow.

cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

This value is passed to f.read() which writes into a 29-byte stack buffer, causing a massive stack buffer overflow.

The diff adds a check 'instr[y].size >= 4' to prevent the unsigned underflow when instr[y].size < 4, which would cause the subtraction 'instr[y].size - 4' to wrap around and result in a massive read into a 29-byte stack buffer. This is precisely the stack overflow described in CVE-2022-34927.

The fix adds a lower bound check (>= 4) before entering the small-header code path.

cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

undesired_operation.description: The parameter k is used as an index into the global array freelist[k] without any bounds check, allowing an out-of-bounds array read/write if k is negative or exceeds the array size.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory access on the freelist array. An attacker who can influence k could read/write arbitrary memory relative to freelist, potentially leading to code execution or crash.

judgments.reasoning: This directly matches CVE-2009-0689: array index error in Balloc where k exceeds the freelist array bounds (Kmax=15). The fix adds exactly this bounds check (k > Kmax).

undesired_operation.description: When k >= (bits in int - 1), the expression '1 << k' causes undefined behavior due to signed integer overflow or shifting past the width of the type. Additionally, for moderately large k (e.g., k=30 on 32-bit), x=1<<k can be very large but (x-1)*sizeof(Long) can overflow, causing a small allocation followed by use as if it were large (heap buffer overflow via undersized MALLOC).
undesired_operation.impact: Integer overflow leads to undersized heap allocation. Subsequent writes to rv->wds entries (up to rv->maxwds) overflow the heap buffer, potentially enabling arbitrary code execution.

x = 1 << k overflows for large k
MALLOC with overflowed size allocates too-small buffer

judgments.reasoning: This is a downstream consequence of the same missing bounds check on k that CVE-2009-0689 describes. The CVE description explicitly mentions 'incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow.' When k exceeds Kmax, not only is freelist accessed OOB, but 1<<k can overflow, leading to the heap buffer overflow described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2009-0689: The Balloc() function in dtoa.c uses parameter k as an index into the fixed-size freelist array (16 elements, Kmax=15) without bounds checking. A large precision value in a printf format string can cause k to exceed Kmax, leading to out-of-bounds array access and potentially incorrect memory allocation resulting in heap buffer overflow. The fix adds a check that k <= Kmax and also adds a NULL check on MALLOC's return value.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

The subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` wraps to a very large value (if hdr.length is unsigned) or becomes negative (if signed), which when passed to pj_strdup causes a massive heap allocation and out-of-bounds read from buf.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or information disclosure — attacker sends a STUN message with an ERROR-CODE attribute whose length field is less than 4, causing pj_strdup to read far beyond the input buffer.

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the integer underflow in `value.slen = attr->hdr.length - 4` when the header length is less than 4, which is exactly the vulnerability described in CVE-2021-37706. The fix adds a check `if (value.slen < 0) value.slen = 0` to prevent this. The CWE-191 matches exactly, and the described impact aligns with the CVE description of potential remote code execution via crafted STUN messages.

actual_issue_summary: In decode_errcode_attr(), the computation `value.slen = attr->hdr.length - 4` does not check whether attr->hdr.length is at least 4 before the subtraction. When an attacker sends a STUN ERROR-CODE attribute with a length less than 4, this results in an integer underflow, causing pj_strdup to operate on an invalid length, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access and remote code execution.

cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

The CWEs CWE-22 (path traversal) and CWE-59 (symlink following) both apply to this CVE.

A malicious zip archive with entries containing path traversal sequences (../) or symbolic links could write files outside the intended extraction directory. The fix adds an isAChildOf check and optionally prevents following symlinks in parent directories.

If the CDEF box has fewer entries than dec->numchans, this results in an out-of-bounds read on the ents array.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure via out-of-bounds heap read, or denial of service via crash

initial_state.value: number of entries in the CDEF box, potentially smaller than dec->numchans

step_by_step_execution.description: dec->numchans is set based on CMAP box or image components count

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: A CDEF box is present in the crafted image

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates using dec->numchans as bound instead of dec->cdef->data.cdef.numchans

out_state.value: increments from 0 up to 3, but ents array only has 2 entries

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix changes the loop bound from dec->numchans to dec->cdef->data.cdef.numchans, which is precisely what this finding identifies as the problem. The CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) also matches.

actual_issue_summary: The loop iterating over CDEF box entries used dec->numchans (derived from CMAP or image components) as the bound instead of dec->cdef->data.cdef.numchans (the actual number of CDEF entries). When the CDEF box has fewer entries than dec->numchans, this causes a heap-based buffer over-read on the ents array.

cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in the bounds check `offsets[tile_idx] + sizeof(int) * 5 > size` allows an attacker-controlled offset value near UINT64_MAX to wrap around to a small value, bypassing the check. Subsequently, `head + offsets[tile_idx]` computes an out-of-bounds pointer, and `memcpy(tile_coordinates, data_ptr, sizeof(int) * 4)` reads from it.
code_snippets: const unsigned char *data_ptr = reinterpret_cast<const unsigned char *>(head + offsets[tile_idx]);
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from attacker-controlled pointer offset. On 32-bit systems, the truncated pointer can point to nearby mapped memory, enabling information disclosure. On 64-bit systems, this typically leads to denial of service via crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: Bounds check performed on tile offset

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Due to integer overflow, the sum wraps to a small value less than size

step_by_step_execution.description: Out-of-bounds pointer computed and used in memcpy

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential integer overflow in the tiled code path's offset bounds check. The actual CVE (CVE-2020-19490) is about a too-large line_no value in the scanline code path causing an integer overflow in DecodePixelData. The fix adds a bounds check on line_no (rejecting values > 2<<20). The finding addresses a completely different code path (tiled vs scanline) and a different variable (offsets vs line_no).

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is an integer overflow caused by an excessively large line_no value read from untrusted input in the scanline decoding path. When line_no is very large (e.g., 2^20 or more), it causes integer overflow in DecodePixelData, leading to out-of-bounds memory writes. The fix adds a validation check rejecting line_no values with absolute value greater than 2<<20 (approximately 2^21).

cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

undesired_operation.impact: Stack-based buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash — attacker controls the pipe path string

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition (CWE-362) in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization sockets. The fix adds a recursive_mutex to protect concurrent access to idInfoMap. The strcpy→strncpy change in PipeSocketHandler is a separate hardening fix included in the same commit but is not the vulnerability described by CVE-2022-24950. The finding describes a buffer overflow, not the race condition.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter where acceptNewConnection() and getInfoForId() access the shared idInfoMap without synchronization. The fix adds a recursive_mutex (routerMutex) with lock_guard to both methods. The strcpy→strncpy change in PipeSocketHandler was a separate hardening fix bundled in the same commit.

cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

undesired_operation.description: gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel are called with x values >= im->sx because the seek-left loop only checks i >= 0 but not i < im->sx. When x >= im->sx, the first iteration accesses pixels beyond the image width.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write beyond image width. An attacker providing x >= im->sx can read/write past the end of a pixel row, corrupting adjacent memory.

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies the missing bounds check on x (and implicitly y) that the fix adds (x >= im->sx clamping and y >= im->sy clamping). While the CVE description emphasizes stack consumption/DoS, the commit fix directly addresses both the out-of-bounds access from unclamped x/y values and the resulting crash. The finding correctly identifies a component of the vulnerability fixed by this commit — the lack of input validation on x and y coordinates that enables the crash described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable gdImageFillToBorder function lacked bounds checking on x and y parameters against image dimensions (im->sx, im->sy). When called with x >= im->sx or y >= im->sy, this could lead to out-of-bounds memory access and, combined with the recursive nature of the fill algorithm, stack consumption causing a crash/DoS. The fix adds clamping of x and y to valid image bounds before processing.

cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

undesired_operation.description: krb5_make_principal return value is not checked in the capath fallback, leaving sp as NULL after it was explicitly set to NULL. The subsequent krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...) dereferences the NULL principal, causing a crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference crash of the KDC process

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2021-3671 is specifically about a missing NULL check on `b->sname` (the `s` variable) when it is not set in the TGS-REQ and `enc_tkt_in_skey` is not requested. The fix adds `else if (s == NULL)` check before dereferencing `*s`. This finding describes a different issue - an unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal in the capath fallback path, which is unrelated to the missing sname validation.

undesired_operation.description: krb5_make_principal return value is not checked in the need_referral fallback path, leaving sp as NULL. The subsequent krb5_unparse_name dereferences the NULL principal, causing a crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference crash of the KDC process

judgments.reasoning: Same as Finding 0 - this describes an unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal in a different code path, not the CVE-2021-3671 issue of missing sname validation in TGS-REQ. The actual CVE is about `s = b->sname` being NULL when no sname is provided and `enc_tkt_in_skey` is not set, leading to a NULL dereference at `*s`.

actual_issue_summary: When a TGS-REQ is received without an sname field and the enc_tkt_in_skey option is not set, the pointer `s` (assigned from `b->sname`) remains NULL. This NULL pointer is then dereferenced at `_krb5_principalname2krb5_principal(context, &sp, *s, r)`, causing a crash. The fix adds an `else if (s == NULL)` check to return an error before the dereference.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

If sparse tensor indices contain negative values, the inner_product computation can produce a negative `loc`, which passes the check but causes an out-of-bounds write via `output_t(loc)`.

undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write before the output tensor buffer. An attacker providing crafted sparse tensor indices with negative values can write to arbitrary memory locations before the output buffer, potentially achieving code execution or causing a crash.

actual_issue_summary: In tf.raw_ops.EditDistance, the computed index `loc` (from inner_product of group indices and output strides) was only checked against the upper bound (`loc < output_elements`) but not the lower bound (`0 <= loc`). Negative sparse tensor indices could produce a negative `loc`, bypassing the check and causing out-of-bounds memory writes. The fix adds `0 <= loc &&` to all five bounds checks.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The loop variable `i` is declared as `int` while `nnz` is `int64_t`. If nnz exceeds INT_MAX, incrementing `i` past INT_MAX causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior), which can lead to an infinite loop or out-of-bounds memory access when the wrapped negative value is used as an index into `indices_mat` and `dense_gathered_flat`.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via infinite loop or crash, potential out-of-bounds read/write when a negative (wrapped) index is used to access tensor data

initial_state.value: indices_t->dim_size(0), could be any int64_t value

Loop begins with int i = 0; i < nnz

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-23567) is about constructing a TensorShape directly from user-provided dimensions via `TensorShape(shape_vec)` without validation, which can cause integer overflow when computing the total number of elements in the shape, leading to large allocations (OOM) or CHECK-failures. The fix replaces this with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape()` which validates dimensions and returns an error status. The finding describes a different issue (int vs int64_t loop variable), which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is in directly constructing a TensorShape from user-provided sp_shape values via `TensorShape(shape_vec)` without validation. Malicious users could provide very large dimension values that cause integer overflow when computing the total element count of the shape, leading to OOM-based denial of service or CHECK-failure crashes. The fix uses `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape()` with proper error checking to validate the shape before use.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

An attacker can craft sparse tensor inputs where the group coordinates and computed strides yield an idx that is negative or exceeds the allocated output tensor size, resulting in an out-of-bounds read/write.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write into the output tensor buffer, potentially leading to denial of service (crash) or arbitrary code execution if an attacker can control the written value and offset.

actual_issue_summary: In sparse reduction operations, the computed flat index from CoordinatesToFlatIndex was used to write to the output tensor without validating that (1) group size matches output_strides size and (2) the index is within bounds of the output tensor. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks for both conditions before the write.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-40575) is a null pointer dereference caused by insufficient data (remain < 5) when the code proceeds to parse headers after dispatching bytes in the 'current > 0' block. The fix adds a 'remain < 5' bounds check before the header parsing section. The finding instead describes a potential heap buffer over-read in the bytes_in_header memcpy at a different code location, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability fixed by the commit. The actual issue is that without the added check, the code can attempt to parse start codes with fewer than 5 bytes remaining, leading to a null pointer dereference.

actual_issue_summary: After dispatching remaining bytes in the 'current > 0' block, the 'remain' variable could drop below 5 bytes. Without a bounds check, the code would proceed to parse headers (reading start codes) with insufficient data, causing a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a 'remain < 5' check before header parsing to store the remaining bytes and break out of the loop.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

The fix adds a check that dense_shape->shape().dim_size(0) == indices->shape().dim_size(1), which would prevent indices from having 0 columns when dense_shape is empty. It also adds dense_shape->NumElements() > 0. Both checks address the scenario described in this finding. While the CVE description focuses on nullptr dereference, the root cause is the same: missing precondition validation on the sparse tensor inputs.

actual_issue_summary: The SparseTensorSliceDataset implementation lacked proper validation of sparse tensor preconditions. The fix adds checks that: (1) values count matches first dimension of indices, (2) dense_shape dimensions match second dimension of indices, and (3) dense_shape has at least one element. Without these checks, crafted inputs could cause undefined behavior including nullptr dereference and out-of-bounds access.

cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

undesired_operation.description: For 1-bit BMP images, colormap index values (0 or 1) are written without bounds checking against image->colors. When number_colors is set to 1, the colormap has only 1 entry (index 0), but pixel data can produce index 1. SyncImage then accesses image->colormap[1] which is an out-of-bounds heap read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read leading to information disclosure of adjacent heap memory or potential crash (denial of service). The leaked heap data would appear as pixel color values in the decoded image.

step_by_step_execution.description: number_colors read from BMP header as 1
image->colors set to bmp_info.number_colors
Pixel data byte has a set bit, producing index 1

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2019-13133 is a memory leak (CWE-401) caused by calling DestroyImageList when AcquireNextImage fails in the multi-image BMP loop, which destroys all previously loaded images without properly closing the blob. The fix changes this to set status=MagickFalse and break, then checks status after the loop to properly clean up. The finding describes an unrelated out-of-bounds colormap access issue, not the memory leak fixed by this commit.

actual_issue_summary: When processing multi-image BMP files, if AcquireNextImage fails (GetNextImageInList returns NULL), the code called DestroyImageList and returned NULL immediately without closing the blob, causing a memory leak. The fix sets status=MagickFalse and breaks out of the loop, then after the loop closes the blob and conditionally destroys the image list, ensuring proper cleanup.
